speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Brand, you told us that when you were a younger man, your hobby was playing the violin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you played in an orchestra. You had an orchestra.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of, of young men. Tell us a little bit about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was during the college times. You know, when I was a child, I was taking music lessons. My mother always insisted I should practice, practice. In fact, when we moved to Brody to high school, I-- we had a, a, a lady teacher. And in other words, I was active.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we decided, when I was in, in the medical school, what are we going to do the whole summer? So we organized a group. You know, there was-- actually, I didn't organize it. But I mean, the pianist was the oldest in the group.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got a, a, a-- five people. And we didn't make much money. But we had lots of fun. You know, young boys, and there was a lake. And we played a few hours a day. We  got food and beer, because there was a brewery in the-- in [INAUDIBLE]. And there's-- that-- and this restaurateur who build this place and also made a profit. So he came every year almost. I had a few pictures somewhere. I don't remember where. But the, the whole group was organized. We had several photographs. In the end when I-- when the war-- everything went, you know, broken out. The war broke out. Nobody was thinking about music. I [? went-- ?] when I walked out from the hospital in Otwock. I left my violin. I left my music library-- extensive, a big one, because I collected music for as long-- since I was a child. And I really-- then when I came to Chicago and I met my wife, and we were, we were talking about music. I said, I used to play. I haven't got a violin. [? My wife ran. ?] And she says you go with me tomorrow, we'll buy a violin. And she paid $400. I didn't have any money, $15. $400 she paid for a vio-- for a, a violin bought in Chicago. And that's the violin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I understand since your wife passed away, you resumed your violin playing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I didn't play for many years, because I didn't-- I was not interested in the violin. I was interested in saving my life, in saving my family, and working, working, working, working. You know, I made house calls. I am this-- in this time of medicine when, when I delivered a, a patient. I didn't have a car, even, when I started in Chicago and with a license. I worked in the Chicago Maternity Center. We delivered about, about-- close to 130 babies I delivered in four months, [INAUDIBLE] of course. And we liked students from Chicago University, because most of them had cars. So you can ride a car instead of waiting for two hours for a street car.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day delivered, a woman on a barge on the Michigan Lake, even-- ridiculous things, yeah, at home, you know? And nobody was sick. There were no infections, nothing. So let's hear you now play.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't guarantee nothing, because I don't see so good, you know? And I'm nervous.

speaker: interviewer
text: We don't need any-- we don't any guarantees.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, boy. [VIOLIN PLAYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Terrible, terrible. What is this piece?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's by Albinoni-- in so-- Albinoni. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] A little more. [PLAYING VIOLIN]

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't play. Well thank you very much. Thank you for playing the violin for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's not playing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you a question. Now your grandchildren are growing up. And you had such a rich-- even though sad-- but very rich life. What is the message you would like to leave your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be Jewish, to be proud of being Jewish. There are many people who are-- there are many people who change their names nowadays, like the Polish Jews changed it. There is no Jewish name any more. There are 5,000 Jews in Poland all together. And everyone has a Jew-- has a Polish name. And not to-- to be good, to do good [? and leave mother ?] before-- think before you do anything. And love, love family. We believe in-- I believe, my wife believed with the whole life, the family is the core of our happiness. And we have to do-- to nurture, to nurture relationships. And don't do  to nobody anything bad that you don't want to be done to yourself. That's all. Mind your own business and keep your nose dry. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the only-- that's my philosophy. I don't, I don't owe anybody a penny. I never cheated anybody. I never was in jail. I take a drink once in a great while. I don't-- I used to smoke. I don't smoke. And I like to fill out my way-- my day, fill out with, with things which satisfy me to do, which are satisfactory to me. And we don't live forever, so get the best out of life if you can. And don't abuse yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know that you have some very interesting pictures that I would like to show.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you again.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is my father. His last name-- his first name is Salamon. He didn't have a middle name. And the last name is Brandwein. And the Russian certificate of birth is wri-- is written 'Braund-wein,' because they didn't know how to spell it. So it's-- actually, it's Brandwein. And I remember him as a good father, never laid a hand on me. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- this picture was taken approximately before he married my mother or right after they married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born 1912, so it must have been 1911 or 1910.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Radzivilov. ?] [? Radzivilov, yeah. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody comes from [? Radzivilov ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. This is a picture of my mother, bless her memory. Her first name was Rachel, [RUSSIAN] in Russian. Rachel [? Baratz ?]. That was her maiden name. And when she married my father, her name was [RUSSIAN], or Rachel, Brand-- Brandwein, B-R-A-N-D-W-E-I-N. And she was studying medicine to a point when she gave up medicine to marry my father-- that the story goes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken before-- it was before 1912-- before 1911.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Radzivilov. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Radzivilov ?] was then what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian-- this was Russia. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a, a, a, a picture of my registration book in the Czech university in Prague, first semester. And that was done in 1930, 1930, first semester before I transferred to the German University. This is the registration book of my studies in the German university of Prague in 1931. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I transferred from German.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is '36 already, 1936.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I mean, I registered in 1931.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's every semester you have to have a new, you know, stamp and everything. So that's starting in 1931 until graduation in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this book is for registering all the sub-- subjects and all the lectures which are required to complete medical studies.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a picture of my times at the-- being a student in the University of Prague, the German University of Prague, walking on main, on the main streets in rare moments when studying was secondary-- facetious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] '32, '33-- '33 probably, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [? you-- ?] and an acquaintance of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Michael Brand, is Michael Brand. And I don't remember the lady's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you registered in school under Michael Brand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [? Mojsiej ?] Brandwein, [? Mojsiej ?] Brandwein-- that's how my cert-- birth certificate said, [? Mojsiej. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture and a certificate taken during the German occupation in about 1942, '40-- 1941-1942. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a-- there was an offer to take-- to work as a-- as a doctor in a village near Brody because of epidemic of typhus fever. And it was required, a certificate of-- and a permit from the Ger-- from the German authorities to be able to do that. And I had to ref-- [? refer ?] the application for a permit to reside in this village every month. And that's my picture. And the years are 1931-- no, 1940-- 1940--

speaker: interviewer
text: Three, probably.

speaker: interviewee
text: '42, '43, yeah, '42, '43-- my name is [? Mojsiej ?] Brandwein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is the Jude scratched out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got mad at, at that and then scratched it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me and my dear wife. And it was taken in, in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here.

speaker: interviewer
text: California?

speaker: interviewee
text: In California, in Ventura, about a year ago in the house-- in the, in the yard by the pool-- my son's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: A year ago means what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: '94, Yeah, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names, both.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name is Anna S. Brand, my wife. My name is Michael Brand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my immediate family taken a year ago-- from left to right, Abraham, close to 17-year-old, his father, Steve Geller, undernea-- and, and below, the daughter-- CREWl Excuse me. I'm sorry. I lost the battery here. This is a picture of my immediate family taken a year ago. So that means 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: '94. From left to right, Abraham Geller, my grandson-- he is 17 years old. Then his father, my son-in-law, Steve Geller, below, kneeling, sitting, my granddaughter Shira Geller. Next is my daughter, Jean Geller. Next to it is my grandson, Benjamin Geller. Next to it is my wife, blessed memory, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anna Brand, and me, Michael Brand, Then Mozel, my granddaughter, my son's family, Mozel, who is about 12 years old, my son, Richard Brand, my daughter-in-law, Ellen Brand, my grandson, Mitchel Brand, and my granddaughter, Rachel Brand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Cambria, in Cambria a year ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: In California.

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in my, in my son's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much again.
